coredata.dc:title: The effect of size and applied electric field on the spin switch in a two-electron graphene quantum dot and graphene qubit
coredata.prism:publicationName: Physica E: Low-dimensional Systems and Nanostructures
coredata.dc:creator: Dong, Qingrui
Yin, Xiaoyu
Liu, Chunxiang
coredata.dc:description: To get a carbon-based qubit, we pay attention to a triangular graphene quantum dot with two electrons occupying the degenerate shell. The spin of the dot is determined by the energy difference between the lowest spin-singlet and spin-triplet states. The energy difference is analyzed as a function of the dot size and an applied electric field. For different sizes, the curves of the energy difference share a similar outline: a brief oscillation as the initial party and a single big peak as the main party. Based on the obtained results, we analyze the spin switches controlled by the weak electric field, as well as by the strong electric field. The weak and strong electric fields have their own advantages in controlling spin switches. When the weak electric field is used for controlling spin switches, the graphene quantum dot with the larger size is available as a qubit. Our findings can be helpful for using a graphene quantum dot as a qubit.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Spin switch
Electric field
Graphene quantum dot
Graphene qubit

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Physica E: Low-dimensional Systems and Nanostructures

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Size effect on the energy difference

ce:caption.ce:simple-para:
TGQD (N
s = 10) subjected to the electric field generated by two gates with electrostatic potentials ± U. The contour of the electrostatic potential is obtained by solving a Laplace equation and shown by grey dashed lines.

[

S = 0

]
and (c) spin S as functions of N
s .

Energy difference ΔE as a function of U for Ns = 4 ~ 11. (Inset) Energy gap E
gap as a function of U for Ns = 8 ~ 11. Every curve is labeled by N
s . As E
gap decreases, the calculation can become unreliable, indicated by the grey shades. The blue shade indicates the quasi-linear region of ΔE in the weak-U region. As a guide for the eyes, the value ΔE = 0 is indicated by the blue dotted line. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Energy differences [ε 2 −ε 1] and
[
E
S = 0
i n t
−
E
S = 1
i n t
]
as functions of U for Ns = 4 ~ 11.

head.ce:title: The effect of size and applied electric field on the spin switch in a two-electron graphene quantum dot and graphene qubit

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Physics and Electronics, Shandong Normal University, Jinan, Shandong, 250014, People's Republic of China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Physics and Electronics, Shandong Normal University, Jinan, Shandong, 250014, People's Republic of China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: To get a carbon-based qubit, we pay attention to a triangular graphene quantum dot with two electrons occupying the degenerate shell. The spin of the dot is determined by the energy difference between the lowest spin-singlet and spin-triplet states. The energy difference is analyzed as a function of the dot size and an applied electric field. For different sizes, the curves of the energy difference share a similar outline: a brief oscillation as the initial party and a single big peak as the main party. Based on the obtained results, we analyze the spin switches controlled by the weak electric field, as well as by the strong electric field. The weak and strong electric fields have their own advantages in controlling spin switches. When the weak electric field is used for controlling spin switches, the graphene quantum dot with the larger size is available as a qubit. Our findings can be helpful for using a graphene quantum dot as a qubit.

ce:section.ce:para: Electron spin is a very promising candidate for a solid-state qubit [1] and some important progresses have been made using semiconductor quantum dots [2–5]. The fabrication of spin-based qubits in solid-state platforms is still a challenge due to the hyperfine interaction of the electron spin with the surrounding nuclear spins [6–8]. Therefore, it is advisable to fabricate qubits in carbon-based quantum dots, where the hyperfine interaction is absent [9,10]. Several studies have been reported for forming qubits using spins of graphene quantum dots (GQDs), such as coupled qubits [11], spin qubits in quantum antidots [12] and electrical spin manipulation [13,14].

It is an essential subtask of making qubits in GQDs to develop the quantitative research on the electronic structure of GQDs. There appears a degenerate shell of zero-energy states in the energy spectra of triangular GQDs (TGQDs) with zigzag edges [15]. This provides a unique opportunity to obtain a strongly correlated electronic system as a function of the shell filling. The ground-state spin of the TGQD is influenced significantly by electron-electron interactions due to strong degeneracy of the degenerate shell. The role of electron-electron interactions in the degenerate shell can be described using a combination of tight-binding, Hartree-Fock, and configuration interaction methods [16]. The accurate calculation of electron-electron interactions allows to study the degenerate shell occupied by two electrons, which is the prerequisite for forming a spin qubit in a two-electron TGQD [17]. The degenerate shell is not sensitive to the change of external magnetic fields [18]. In contrast, an external electric field can lead to a linear splitting of the degenerate shell, which provides an opportunity for the control of a TGQD-based qubit [19].

In this paper, we concentrate on the TGQD with two electrons filled in the degenerate shell. We apply an electric field to the TGQD and the aim is to form a qubit using the ground-state spin of the dot. The spin of the dot is determined by the energy difference between the lowest spin-singlet and spin-triplet states. Thus the energy difference is analyzed in detail as a function of the TGQD size and the applied electric field. In our previous work, we proposed a scheme for controlling two electrons in the degenerate shell [20]. Moreover, a singlet-triplet spin switch in a TGQD with a specific size can be controlled by a stronger electric field [14]. As an extension of these works, we now investigate the effect of the TGQD size on the singlet-triplet spin switch. The results show that the weaker electric field can be used for controlling spin switches and that the TGQD with the larger size can be used as a qubit.

In Fig. 1 , an in-plane electric field is applied to a TGQD with the size N
s = 10, where N
s is the number of carbon atoms in each edge of the dot. The electric field is generated by two gate electrodes with opposite electrostatic potentials ± U. To calculate the eigenstates for the TGQD with two electrons filled in the degenerate shell, the three steps are carried out as follows: First, the single-electron states are calculated with a fully occupied valence band and a completely empty degenerate shell; Second, the set of basis vectors for the two-electron Hamiltonian is constructed with obtained single-electron states; Third, the two-electron energy spectra are calculated.

At the first step of the calculation, based on the tight-binding method, a Hartree-Fock self-consistent calculation is carried out to obtain the single-electron states of the TGQD. In the tight-binding method, the electric field plays a role by adding local external electrostatic potentials into on-site energies. By the Hartree-Fock calculation, the edge effect and the electron-electron interaction are considered [21]. Due to the large gap between the valence band and the degenerate shell, the closed-shell system of the valence electrons can be well described in a mean-field approximation. The corresponding Hamiltonian can be expressed as [14].
(1)
H
=
∑
i σ
U
i
c
i σ
+
c
i σ
+
∑
i l σ
t
i l
c
i σ
+
c
l σ
+
∑
i l σ
∑
j k
σ
′
(
ρ
j k
σ
′
−
ρ
j k
σ
′
o
)
(
⟨ i j
| V |
k l ⟩ − ⟨ i j
| V |
l k ⟩
δ
σ
σ
′
)
c
i σ
+
c
l σ
where U
i is the gate electrostatic potential at site i,
c
i σ
+
(c
iσ ) creates (annihilates) an electron with spin σ, t
il is a hopping integral, ρ
jkσ′ is the density matrix element for the TGQD,
ρ
j k
σ
′
o
is for the charge-neutral bulk graphene, and ⟨ij|V|kl⟩ is the two-body Coulomb matrix element. However, the mean-field Hartree-Fock method can not correctly captured the interactions between electrons filled in the degenerate shell except for the charge-neutral system [22]. Nevertheless, the Hartree-Fock method is still valid for the TGQD with a fully occupied valence band and a completely empty degenerate shell. At the third step, the exact diagonalization for the two-electron Hamiltonian matrix is carried out, which is the most accurate and direct way of solving the many-body Schrödinger equation. The two-electron Hamiltonian matrix may be written as [14].
(2)
H =
H
1
+
H
2
+
V
i n t
(
|
r
2
−
r
1
|
)
,
where H 1 (H 2) is the single-electron Hamiltonian with the formalism of Eq. (1) and V
int (| r
2 − r
1 |) is the interaction between two electrons. The exact-diagonalization method captures all correlation effects missing in Hartree-Fock and density functional theory calculations. Here, the two-electron spin S is a good quantum number and the obtained two-electron eigenstates span two independent subspaces with S = 0 and S = 1, respectively. The more details for our methodology and all material parameters can be found in Ref. [14].

In the two-electron degenerate shell obtained, we focus on the energy difference ΔE between the lowest S = 0 and S = 1 states, which determines the ground-state spin of the two-electron system. To get an insight into the energy difference ΔE, it is necessary to analyze in detail the energy
E
S = 0
0
of the lowest S = 0 state and the energy
E
S = 1
0
of the lowest S = 1 state. The energy
E
S = 0
0
can be decomposed in two parts [14]: (1) the energy of two non-interacting electrons occupying the lowest single-electron state, 2ε 1; (2) the interaction energy of the two electrons filled in the shell,
E
S = 0
i n t
. Thus, one gets
(3)
E
S = 0
0
= 2
ε
1
+
E
S = 0
i n t
.
Similarly, one also gets
(4)
E
S = 1
0
=
ε
1
+
ε
2
+
E
S = 1
i n t
,
where ε 1 + ε 2 is the energy of two non-interacting electrons occupying the two lowest single-electron states. Thus, the energy difference ΔE can be expressed as
(5)
Δ E
=
E
S = 0
0
−
E
S = 1
0
=
(
E
S = 0
i n t
−
E
S = 1
i n t
)
−
(
ε
2
−
ε
1
)
.
We will mainly use Eq. (5) to analyze the energy difference ΔE later.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Size effect on the energy difference

ce:section.ce:para:
We first observe the effect of the TGQD size on the energy difference ΔE in the absence of an electric field. As the size increases, the energy difference ΔE oscillates and tends to converge to zero, as shown in Fig. 2 (a). This can be explained by analyzing the changes of the terms [ε 2 −ε 1] and
[
E
S = 0
i n t
−
E
S = 1
i n t
]
in Eq. (5). As N
s increases, both [ε 2 −ε 1] and
[
E
S = 0
i n t
−
E
S = 1
i n t
]
oscillate and tend to decrease, as shown as Fig. 2(b). Moreover, both the two terms tend to converge to zero. As a single-electron level spacing, the term [ε 2 −ε 1] tends to decrease as N
s increases, which is reasonable agreement with the weakening of quantum confinement effect. As the quantum confinement becomes weaker, the two-electron interaction energies and their differences, including the term
[
E
S = 0
i n t
−
E
S = 1
i n t
]
, tend to decrease, which is also reasonable behavior. Meanwhile, as the size increases, the system's geometry is significantly changed and thus the states also significantly change. The change of the states causes a significant disturbance to the decreasing processes of the terms [ε 2 −ε 1] and
[
E
S = 0
i n t
−
E
S = 1
i n t
]
and thus the two terms decrease in oscillation. The competition between the weakening of quantum confinement effect and the change of the states, leads that the energy difference ΔE oscillates and tends to be zero.

The ground-state spin S can be directly extracted according to the sign of the difference ΔE. As the size increases, the ground-state spin S oscillates between 0 and 1, as shown in Fig. 2(c). It seems that the spin S tends to be zero as N
s increases. However, the finding is worthless for designing a qubit since the degeneracy of the S = 0 and S = 1 states occurs simultaneously.

Now we observe the dependence of the energy difference ΔE on the electrostatic potential U for different TGQD sizes, as shown in Fig. 3 . Although for different sizes, all ΔE-U curves share a similar outline described as follows. For U < 0.1 eV, all curves oscillate with only one oscillation peak [14]. Then all curves rise to a rather large peak value. And then, all curves drop steadily and for smaller sizes the curves drop down to a negative value. Fig. 4 (a) shows that all [ε 2 −ε 1] curves undergo two stages: oscillation and linear splitting. Fig. 4(b) shows that all
[
E
S = 0
i n t
−
E
S = 1
i n t
]
curves undergo three stages: oscillation, transition and stability [14]. According to Eq. (5), the shared outline of the ΔE curves can be explained by combining the behaviors of the
[
E
S = 0
i n t
−
E
S = 1
i n t
]
and [ε 2 −ε 1] curves.

In addition to the shared outline, as the size increases, the peak position of the ΔE-U curve moves to the right and the peak value decreases. To understand this phenomenon, we observe the behaviors of [ε 2 −ε 1] and
[
E
S = 0
i n t
−
E
S = 1
i n t
]
, respectively. Fig. 4 shows that both the [ε 2 −ε 1] curve and the
[
E
S = 0
i n t
−
E
S = 1
i n t
]
curve rise more slowly as N
s increases, which is attributed to the weakening of quantum confinement effect. According to Eq. (5), the behaviors of [ε 2 −ε 1] and
[
E
S = 0
i n t
−
E
S = 1
i n t
]
jointly lead to the change of the ΔE-U curve with the size of the TGQD.

It should be noted that the results in the grey shades of Fig. 3 can be unreliable, which can be explained from the perspective of the calculational method. The validity of the Hartree-Fock method in our calculation is based on the assumption that there is a large energy gap E
gap between the valence band and the degenerate shell. However, for the TGQDs with different sizes, the energy gaps E
gap decrease as U increases, as shown in the inset of Fig. 3. Thus, the Hartree-Fock method becomes invalid as U increases, which is a gradual process. It is difficult to find a critical value of E
gap for the invalidity. Nevertheless, for the convenience of analysis, we still suppose that the critical value of E
gap is 0.3 eV. According to the critical value, for the TGQD with N
s = 9, the Hartree-Fock method is no longer valid at around U > 1.0 eV. Similarly, for the TGQDs with N
s = 10 and 11, the critical value of U are about 0.9 eV and 0.75 eV, respectively.

For using the two-electron spin of a TGQD as the qubit, the key is to control the spin switch between 0 and 1 by the external electric field. Whether in the weak-U region (U < 0.1 eV) or in the strong-U region (U > 0.4 eV), there exist several spin switches, as shown in Fig. 3. First, we analyze the spin switches in the weak-U region. For U < 0.1 eV, the oscillation of the energy differences ΔE with U seems messy but contains opportunities for controlling spin switches. For N
s = 5, 6 and 8, the spin always remains S = 1 in the weak-U region, which excludes the possibility of a spin switch. For each of the sizes (N
s = 4, 7, 9, 10 and 11), there exists a desirable spin switch in the weak-U region. Although these spin switches are close to the oscillation region, the energy differences ΔE are nearly linear with U about the corresponding switch point, as shown in Fig. 3. This quasi-linearity is helpful to the operation of a qubit. The result means that the weak electric fields (U < 0.1 eV) can control spin switches, which is not found in the previous work [14]. Second, we analyze the spin switches in the strong-U region. For each of the sizes (Ns = 4 ~ 9), there exists a desirable spin switch. For N
s = 10 and 11, there is not a desirable spin switch in the strong-U region. This result does not exclude the possibility of a spin switch since here the calculation can be unreliable.

The calculated results suggest that the weak and strong electric fields have their own advantages in controlling spin switches. One advantage of the weak electric field is to allow for the use of the larger-size TGQD as a qubit, which can reduce the difficulty of making a qubit. Another potential advantage of the weak electric field is a reduction of the interference between gate voltages and source/drain voltages. One advantage of the strong electric field is that the quasi-linear regions of ΔE are larger. Another advantage of the strong electric field is that the energy differences ΔE can get larger values both for S = 0 and for S = 1. The two advantages are helpful for the stability of a graphene qubit.

One should note that it is also possible to control the spin using a perpendicular magnetic field along with the electric field. With the applied magnetic field, the two-electron energy is composed of two parts: the energy related to the spatial wave function and the Zeeman coupling of the magnetic field to the spin. However, previous work reported that the degenerate shell was immune to the magnetic field [18]. If the Zeeman term is added to the Hamiltonian, the energy of the spin-triplet state is lowered by about 15 meV with a magnetic field intensity of 10 T and the spin-singlet energy is unaltered. This can change the spin when the ground state is a spin-singlet state separated by a small gap (comparable with the Zeeman energy) from the excited spin-triplet state.

We have studied the two-electron degenerate shell of the TGQD subjected to the external electric field. To use the spin of the TGQD as a qubit, we focus on the dependence of the energy difference ΔE on the size and on the electric field. In the absence of an electric field, with the increase of the size, the energy difference ΔE strongly oscillates and tends to converge to zero, and thus the ground-state spin oscillates between 0 and 1. For different sizes, the ΔE-U curves share a similar outline: a brief oscillation as the initial party and a single big peak as the main party. Based on these results, we analyze the spin switches controlled by the weak electric field, as well as by the strong electric field. When the weak electric field is used to control spin switches, the TGQD with the larger size is available as a qubit. The calculated results suggest that the weak and strong electric fields have their own advantages in controlling spin switches. Our findings can be helpful for using a graphene quantum dot as a qubit of a carbon-based quantum computer.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 11604183), and a Project of Shandong Province Higher Educational Science and Technology Program (Grant No. J16LJ09).

F. H. Koppens, C. Buizert, K.-J. Tielrooij, I. T. Vink, K. C. Nowack, T. Meunier, L. Kouwenhoven and L. Vandersypen, Nature, 2006, 442, 766.

B. Trauzettel, D. V. Bulaev, D. Loss and G. Burkard, Nature Physics, 2007, 3, 192.

M. Ezawa, Phys. Rev. B, 2007, 76, 245415.
A. D. Guclu, P. Potasz, M. Korkusinski and P. Hawrylak, Graphene quantum dots, Springer, 2014.
A. Harju, S. Siljamaki and R. M. Nieminen, Phys. Rev. Lett., 2002, 88, 226804.
A. D. Guclu, P. Potasz and P. Hawrylak, Physical Review B, 2013, 88, 155429.
Q. Dong, F. Jiang, S. Lian and C. Liu, Journal of Magnetism and Magnetic Materials, 2019, 476, 225-228.
W.-d. Sheng, M. Korkusinski, A. D. Guclu, M. Zielinski, P. Potasz, E. S. Kadantsev, O. Voznyy and P. Hawrylak, Frontiers of Physics, 2012, 7, 328-352.
J. Fernandez-Rossier and J. J. Palacios, Phys. Rev. Lett., 2007, 99, 177204.
